prompt: From 'P → Q' and '¬P', what can you validly conclude?
options: Nothing — this is the fallacy of denying the antecedent
hint: P → Q does not say P is the ONLY way to get Q. If P is false, is Q forced to be false?
explanation: You cannot conclude ¬Q. 'P → Q' plus '¬P' is the fallacy of DENYING THE ANTECEDENT — Q can still be true through some other route even though P is false. The valid cousin is Modus Tollens: from 'P → Q' and '¬Q', conclude ¬P.
